title: What do you find most appealing about the Eva pilots
display_title: What do you find most appealing about the Eva pilots
wikitext:
[]: What do you find most appealing about the Eva-pilots?             Quote Post by Write » Fri Dec 21, 2012 5:31 am I'm writing a story (I have aspirations for the story but they're unimportant right now) and I'd like to create the most appealing characters possible, even if only by design. I hope I get a lot of replies. Evangelion's fan base is particularly important to me as my story has it's share of [indirect] influences. It's not mecha or anything like that, but it's worth noting that I was inspired by Hideaki Anno and the love people have for his characters when they're not inherently appealing (or maybe they are, that's why I'm asking).

[]:             Quote Post by A.T. Fish » Fri Dec 21, 2012 6:37 am The boobs. Also, in the case of Shinji, the way he so accurately displays the behavior of a depressed person is probably the greatest asset the character has. Rei is shrouded enough in mystery to keep your attention. Asuka becomes a very interesting character only when you discover her past, until then she comes off as irritating, but, you know, boobs.

[]:             Quote Post by Darkwing » Fri Dec 21, 2012 9:18 am Hmm, I guess I'd say vulnerability? It's that they really aren't supermen who can just man up and save the day, being awesome. They have these deep scars and painful pasts, and it isn't just in the past, it shapes them. Rei is an intersting charecter because she really isn't someone who has had a life, to the point she didn't even realize it. She only just realizes by the time she dies, but that makes it more poignient. Shinji is an accurate look at what the kind of trauma he'd go through would do to someone, but that just makes me want to see him grow beyond it more. Asuka, I didn't like at first. It wasn't until her crying "momma" in her sleep that I liked the charecter, because she really was a lot like Shinji, but can't admit it. You actually do want to see them grow and gain happiness. Now don't assume that it's a case of "oh look at the poor woobie, I wish they were happy". It's not just because of them having trauma and sad pasts. It's seeing them grow and what happens to them. It's that they have enough depth you want to care about them, rather than doing so just because their the main charecters.

[]:             Quote Post by Nyanlathotep » Fri Dec 21, 2012 12:30 pm A good phrase from Anno describes what I like about them: They have blood. Like, even in most other stories where the characters are emotionally damaged, there are still bits and pieces of their personalities that are subdued so as to make the characters inoffensive and easy to watch. The characters, in their emotional pains, can get really hard to watch, just like watching a good friend have an emotional breakdown. Anno, more than any other director I've seen, nails the pathos of very disturbed characters. This is because the characters are portrayed extremely honestly, and while there is depth to them, a lot of that depth ultimately comes to the surface in a powerful way as the characters reach their boiling points. Also, Anno has a knack for punishing character judgment and hatred. Think Asuka's a bitch? Here's why she's the way she is; I hope you have your Zoloft ready. Think Gendou is cold and manipulative because he's a psychopath? Psyche! His mind has been ruined by an obsession with his dead* wife and a crippling fear of connecting with anyone else-- he simply tries to cope with his duress by treating the people around him as objects.

[]:             Quote Post by Rei IV » Fri Dec 21, 2012 12:54 pm They all suffer from some form of self-hatred and have identity issues to boot. Some of the character's deficiencies are also very similar which brings back to their self hatred.

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Fri Dec 21, 2012 1:10 pm They're all flawed. That's something too many authors forget to do when writing characters. They get so wrapped up in trying to make them likable that they forget to add flaws, they become too two dimensional, and the result is they're less likable because of it. Just don't feel obligated to fuck your characters up as much as Anno did.

[]:             Quote Post by Tribblepoo » Fri Dec 21, 2012 3:22 pm CJD wrote: They're all flawed. That's something too many authors forget to do when writing characters.

[]:             Quote Post by Darkwing » Fri Dec 21, 2012 5:41 pm CJD wrote: They're all flawed. That's something too many authors forget to do when writing characters. They get so wrapped up in trying to make them likable that they forget to add flaws, they become too two dimensional, and the result is they're less likable because of it. Just don't feel obligated to fuck your characters up as much as Anno did.

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Fri Dec 21, 2012 6:25 pm I figured "including virtues too" went without saying guys. How often do you encounter a character who has no virtues? Compare that to how often you encounter a Mary Sue? Including virtues is easy, most people do it by default, the hard part is including believable flaws. Going off "believable," I'd like to reiterate something I've said before (most recently the other day): The difference between Shinji and the angst ridden protagonists in a lot of shows lately is that Shinji has a reason to act the way he does. Shinji's issues are believable because of his past. Don't just throw in flaws for the sake of flaws, make them believable character traits.

[]:             Quote Post by Nyanlathotep » Fri Dec 21, 2012 6:26 pm Not only do they have flaws, they have real flaws-- flaws that can't just be brushed aside or conveniently reconciled. That's another thing writers and narrative artists tend to forget. EDIT: Also, going from above-- the characters also have realistic and flaws. A big goal of art in general is that you want everything to come across as justified within the story.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Fri Dec 21, 2012 6:30 pm I guess first of all, it's the sheer level of detail and artistic beauty Anno put into them. Down to philosophic opinions and hobbies and everything, and it all fits together/tells you something about them/ flows from the same basic traits. There are just many aspets/layers of meanings to them, just like a real person is not "just one thing", you can really get a feel for their inner universes... A problem with creating characters is the balance between making them represent things, abstract concepts for your message and still feel like real people - Obviously, Anno aced that department, as much as the mystery/relatability axis or the question of what and how much to reveal. Realism has been mentioned before - Eva's writing is just very lifelike, I often heard the dialogue and thought "Heck, I had that same conversation with my mother/classmates a while ago!". They're modelled after basic types of people that our modern sucess-oriented society produces, and obviously, the audience may recognize these types, leading to strong reactions of "That poor girl/boy is just like me! I feel your pain, [insert character here]! " or "Yes, I know this kind of asshole from my own life and I can't stand them!" Sometimes, they might do things you wouldn't expect of them, and that challenges you to remedy your model/theory of their inner mechanics (For me, it was mostly Shinji I had that experience with, but many mention having it with Asuka) One trick is probably that he gave most of the characters, not just the pilots, a mixture of very positive and very negative traits, or traits that can both positive and negative depending on the situation they come out, not the usual 2-3 good and one bad trait model used most frequently. In general, an apparent contradiction can be a good thing to base an interesting, memorable character about. (For example, Sadamoto (or was it Anno? Not sure, i think it was the former) once said that his first idea of Ritsuko was "a miniskirt half-concealed under a lab-coat". Or take Gendo. A hard, extremely pragmatic, un-sentimental leader ... who regurlarly visits an empty grave. Misato, the cool, professional, hypercompetent pretty babe... who's a drunkyard living in a filthy appartment. Shinji. Recklessly charges the monster against direct orders, puts a knife in it the most primal way possible, and just keeps going no matter what that thing does to him, screaming like a mad ape, taking it down at the last second, also saving two involving civilians and the rest of the planet ...and then breaks down crying, reduced to a complete wreck. ) As far as the pilots in particular are concerned, well, why'd Anno use 14-year-olds. Simple: Not too old to angst over parents, old enough to angst about misadventures with the opposite sex. And because they're still naturally in a process of develeloping, that makes them ideal for a story about a "journey" In addition to that, they all sorta have their own appeal, after all, not everyone likes them all and some like some of them while strongly disliking certain others. The people who like both Shinji and Asuka probably make for one small list XD Shinji: a) Realism. As mentioned before, that's a factor with most of EVAs cast, but Shinji is the ordinary PoV character who gets dragged into that weird world of mecha battles. He's so very, very human, for better or for worse. And his struggles/flaws are very accurate depictions of what some actual unhappy teenagers go through. People are gonna recognize that, and react. b) Fragility appeal. Poor thing, don't you feel like hugging him just from listening to his voice for too long? He's such a nice, guy, too, and even if he wavers, even if he considers quitting, in the end, he keeps continueing his struggles despite the pain. That makes it way more satisfying to see him succeed at something than your average hero, because it's a lot harder to him and will mean a lot more to/for him. c) Conversely, he still manages to keep some mysterious blanks in his backstory and has a very unpredictable quality to him, a hidden core of anger, something powerful and abyssal, terrifying that's fascinating and awesome to behold, but may just be something you can't coexist with. So he has the appeal of a fairly repressed, clean and nice-seeming guy with a hidden black soul lurking in his depths. d) Most non-stereotypical bisexual character ever. Rei (mostly second version) a) Fragility appeal, once again. The basic feeling of maybe being needed, maybe being able to win the loyalty of a even a distant-seeming person if you only try and show them kindness. The instinct to protect. She evokes that like no other, and it helps that we see the story through the eyes of someone who feels the same way about her. Her VA always makes her sound like she's about to faint, we see her sad, sad living conditions and the terrible knowledge she's forced to live with. She care's far too little about herself and her own wishes, her very life, her modesty, and so, you want to do it for her. b) She's a challenge for your brainz. Piecing together what the deal is, all the philosophic mojo involved, what we see her ask herself, and what she makes us ponder. The meaning of identity, communication and hope, basically. c) Were you ever a real life socially clueless young girl? Rei's life is gonna seem like a big metaphor for yours. Several scenes there felt ripped right out of my life.... "I know that pain"-sort of appeal, once again. d) There's also the expressionist concept of your life an an individual human feeling insignificant in the modern world, the coldness and sterility of science/civilization/ big cities, the anonimity and the feeling that it wouldn't really matter if one person more or less were there. e) But conversely, seeing her story may convince you that your life can be improved if it sucks like hers. She has worse reasons for her life sucking and her feeling the way she does than you probably have, but even she found a friend. And she manages to be a kind, determined, honest and loyal person despite it all. That makes her someone to admire, I guess, that silent streght she has. She worries about her teammates and holds no grudges, but will not suffer her loved ones being insulted. She's honest, to the point of being brutal with it, she doesn't hide herself and that's beautiful. Her personality also makes her a cute and likeable character that you want to see happy, I guess. The appeal of the third version is mostly of the SciFi/ philosophic sort, but her character arc also has aspects comparable to the human wish/struggle for freedom from opression and going one's own way/ expression of free will, or a daghter's wish to be independent from her father and his plans for her, and it's strangely powerful to see such a very human, relatable sentiment in a mostly in-human character who sees her existence as a wrong, undesirable state in a way we can't understand. An interesting exploration how she's mentally/metaphisically a half-human-hybrid, not just in body. The whole theme with the clones and how they differ despite having the same bodies/ same memories is also a nice exploration of what makes a human identity and what happens when you twist the various ingedients. It's beautiful, it's just beautiful, how both mayor clones were ultimately just themselves, not Lillith, not Yui, not just their upbringing in a lab, not just the purpose intended for them by Gendo. ReiIIs connection with Shinji was ultimately hers and hers alone, a teenaged girl becoming friends with or developing a crush on another teenager, drawn together because they knew the same loneliness. Yui or Lillith would see him as their son, not as someone equal or a source of comfort, and certainly not a potential love interest. And ReiIIIs final choice to defy Gendo was also hers, not her predecessors, hers alone. Even if her choice entailed giving up her indentity, it was hers... Fuck, I'm getting teary-eyed just writing about it. You see? Beautiful. Kaworu: a) Intelligent, kind, devoted, can read people like books and seems to understand everything. He's hot, and he likes music and philosophy. What's not to like? Don't we all wish to meet such a person? b) His musings about human nature, it's limitations, but also the beauty in it. I was never the same after listening to them. He just looks like such a shining light when he appears in the dark, empty world of evangelion's fourth act, so positive, so enlightened, and he, a stranger from the outside who's not part of humanity, makes you appreciate it through new eyes. Episode 24 makes my soul happy. It makes me wish that maybe I could be like him, it makes me want to try. Because he was used and betrayed by SEELE, he's an artificial being just like Rei, he's agonizing about big choices just like Shinji, he knows his days in the world around him are numbered, but instead of agonizing over it like the rest of the cast, he choses to enjoy what he has, he goes forward and connects to people. I find Kaworu Nagisa inspiring beyond words and i tell you, episode 24 changed my life. c) I guess also SciFi/cerebral reasons, as with Rei. Mari: a) Somewhat like Kaworu, there are implications that her past sucked, but she has a positive attitude. She knows that she's weird, but she has no complex about it, and got herself a situation where she can be the way she is and make use of it, too. b) Bad. Ass. I usually don't care for fight scenes, but... it's not even that she always wins or is super skilled, it's the way she's ready tp utilize everything in her grasp. c) Subversive Element in a way perfect for the setting. In a world full of lies and secrets, she is the one who knows, and escapes the tangles webs of fate, unlike the people who keep walking right into them... d) That "dissonant serenity" of hers. Asuka: ...Hm, I guess someone who actually likes her in an emotional way can explain that better than me. Here's what I see about her: a) Escapism. She tells people around her they suck without a moments hesitantion and is very complainy. Half of us wish we could, but we're afraid of the repercussions. So watching her can provise catharsis, especially if the plot untill episode 8 annoyed you. ...I guess. I can't like her, the way she treats Rei reminds me so much of when I used to be bullied when I was younger, but I can see her more cerebral attempts, the point of this character, and the message. IT's comparable to Marina's "Electra Heart" album, I guess. This character adresses issues present in our society, she represents a type or types of people that our society produces, with the drive to be perfect, beautiful and popular. Surrounded by pictures of "perfect" bodies, ware that there's always someone better for work and school. b) She has to deal with the common situation of her parent's divorce, a mother who's said to have put her work before her and ultimstely dies for it, leaving Asuka behind, not recognizing her. It's probably an exxageration for, well, workaholic mothers than don't die, but still don't pay much attention to their children. And the whole "Daddy doesn't love mommy anymore. Does that mean he regrets having me"-complex and the stepmother situation ("Want to have a mother, but feel like I have to reject that person because she represents Daddy's betrayal") I guess that if your parents divorced and you didn't agree with it (I'd be happy if my mother finally got rid of her asshole husband, more like Misato) , too, episodes 22 and 25 are gonna make you bawl, dunno. In any case, it's adressing a problem people may know. c) Her whole perfectionism complex, and breakdown it leads to. Asuka is essentially someone who gets themselves a burnout syndrome.It's a problem people have. She makes rivals of perople who were never too different from her and could've been allies. She's a lone fighter, she feels like it's her against the world and of course it hurts/is too much for her to take on the whole word. She needs to be perfect, and so, she breaks what could have been not perfect, but good. she could do it all better if she just relaxed, but something happened to her to make her need the recognition of being elite, to crave the stagelight and attention like a drug, and it becomes a vicious circle. d) related to the drivbe to be perfect in a professional/academic way, but still very different, the drive to be "socially" perfect, to have a perfect body. She seems very petty and superficial at first, concerded with silly things like "winning" or "appearances" and making fun of the "losers" around her, and sometimes she gets very cruel there, but it's only to mask her insecurities, as it it often in real life. She's projecting a fake image that prevents her connections from being meaningful, because no one knows her real self, so it'd doesn't really help her loneliness. Asuka is shown to be vain, ("Look at my curves!""No way I'm wearing that silly suit..." ) and she stands out as being very thin in an anime where the drawing styles makes everyone noodles to begin with, and she ends up gaunt and emaciated, in that filthy bathtub. Compare/contrast classic story of you average anorexia victim. She's that "alpha bitch" girl who goes and viciously bullies outsiders like Rei, and pushes around less confident individuals (like Shinji) to do her bidding, but as in real life bullying, there's a lot of projecting involved. Episode 25: "You are trying to find yourself in others..." She sees weakness in herself, but she can't accept it, so she hates others who remind her of it, hates it in others. She looks for people who seem to be more miserable than her to make herself feel better. Of course, this makes her have very simplistic, carricaturized perceptions of others. She assigns them roles in "her" story, the tragicomic play that her life is and where even she is an actress playing the role of a simplistic carricature, and so, she doesn't get to know their real selves, doesn't get to see their real feelings that she is hurting. She calls Misato a slut, Shinji a weakling and Rei an obedient mechanical puppet, but in truth, it's Asuka herself who has self-doubts and is afraid of being a slut, a weakling or an obedient mechanical puppet. She wants to reject these things, separate them from herself. e) But it's also the story of a naive young girl who thought she was prepared shattering upon the hard rocks of reality and to come to term with being a woman of all sudden. She acts as if she were confident with her role and her appeal,, giving her firsts away like they're something shameful to have, not something precious to keep, but in fact, she's insecure about her ability to win the hearts of men and fails to connect with people who are already women and could guide her. She's ve ry childish and selfish in some ways, but she doesn't really realize. She was a child-woman complex, throwing herself on an older man, but in the end, she's a lonely little girl who failed to become a woman. She's uncomfortable with her own ability to give life. Or partially suceeds in 2.0, it's a matter of debate, and don't get me started on Q, but yeah. That part of the character gets adressed in a different way there. You could say that EoE has her come to full circle, or that it doesn't, but that's a theme there. So in many ways, she's very relevant, and I guess that makes her appealing... again, I tried my best but you should better ask an Asuka fan thereXD I absolutely love the other four, though. I love them to bits. But I guess part of the point is, a character that adresses relevant issues is more likely to be good and make the fans talk about them.

[]:             Quote Post by Darkwing » Fri Dec 21, 2012 9:36 pm Actually I think the pilots being 14 years old was because most Mecha protagonists are about that age. Well that's what it says on the bio. They don't often act that way. And that was one of the major deconstructions of Eva. You actually put 14 year old kids in these things, what's going to happen, and whats it going to do to them. How it'll put pressure on them. And where they'll break. Now for me the episodes between Asuka's arrival and Leliel are actually important. There were we get to just see everyone being themselves. There's some little charecter moments (though I think Rei could have used a few more), and we do learn a little bit. It's the calm before the storm. It gives us a chance to get comfortable with the charecters before it all goes to hell. If you don't care about the charecters you won't feel for them when they get put through hell. And lets face it if the creators did a good job then you will care about the charecters, for good or ill. -Shinji. At times I want to hug the kid, tell him he did good and that he's a good person, and he just needs to keep working on things, he'll get it eventually. Other times I want to scream at him in frustration because he's such a screw up, and yell at him that his father is not worth getting emotionally eaten alive over. -Rei. She's the one I want to see bloom. Come out into the world and find out what it means to be happy. To see not just smile, but laugh. And in some ways she's the saddest charecter to me because it never has a chance to happen. It gets nipped in the bud right as it was begining. As for Rei 3, there is always a sense of unease. But at the same time I wish she'd understand that death is not something to long for or to fear. -Asuka. Now here's someone at times I want to choke. But I really do like to see it in those rare times when she allows herself to be something other than the alpha bitch who is invincible. Not just her vulnerable moments, but her very rare kind moments. Like deciding they were going for Ramen instead of steak so Rei could join them. I want to scream at her "Of course no one likes you! You don't like yourself! Stop trying to force everything so you can pretend it doesn't hurt!" And of course at the same time I feel for her, that poor little girl who didn't just lose her mother, but saw her descend into insanity. And who was smart enough to know what the doll means... -Toji. The dumb jock. Only he's not. On the surface I probably would never have hung out with a jock like him in highschool. or maybe I would have, since Kensuke does. He comes across like a dumb lunk at first. But really he's got so many good traits. He loves his sister very much. He has a sense of honor. He hit Shinji when Shinji didn't deserve it, so he needs Shinji to hit him back. He has that kindness Hikari sees, and that attracts her. And then there is a great scene that sadly is only in the manga. He tells Shinji that he's the 4th child and breaks down in tears because he's so scared. And really any kid his age suddenly going into that should. Espescially since he's seen what it's like for Shinji. -Misato. A damn capable operations officier, and as we see in EOE, a dangerous woman. But at the same time she's as bad in her personal life as the children. She doesn't know if she loves or hates her father. She managed to screw up her relationship with the man she loves because of that. Her best freind? At times the two can't stand each other, and really she doesn't know her that well. The scar on her chest isn't the only one she's got. -Kawrou. If I had to liken him to any other fictional character I'd say The Doctor. For whatever reason he loves humanity. And he loves Shinji who really embodies the human condition. But at the same time he's always sort of distant and different. Of all the angels, only he really resembles what most people would call an angel, and I don't just mean looks. -Gendo. Oh don't get me started. I'll be here all night. Suffice it to say I find him both pityable and pitifule. Sympathetic, and contemptable. But it says tons that I can find so very much to say about the characters.

[]:             Quote Post by Clover » Fri Dec 21, 2012 10:35 pm They all meet horrible ends from their own decisions.

[]:             Quote Post by Seele00TextOnly » Fri Dec 21, 2012 10:46 pm ...

[]:             Quote Post by Eva-UnitK » Sat Dec 22, 2012 3:48 am Well what I find most appealing is how realistic the characters feel and how I can relate to them. Shinji: Shinji is basically a perfect representation of how I was in my early days of adolescence. Like him I felt like I had no freedom on anything and pretty much didn't care about my own existence. I was angry the whole time during those years and just kept my feelings bottled up and never consulted with anyone for help. So I completely understands his reasons for his actions and I also understand his own hatred of himself. Asuka: The reason why I like Asuka so much is because of her ability to keep moving forward and still have the will to live even after witnessing what she's been through. She never backs away and always tries to be in the spotlight all the time in order to show the world that she exists. Heck she even went as far as finishing school in Germany all the way to her college graduation! She desperately wanted to be known and she also felt that she needs to do these things to praise herself and erase the sadness in her heart. Though probably more mentally unstable than the other pilots are she still tried to be the most normal of the trio to be around. Something that I couldn't do even if I tried. She doesn't want to be a insignificant nobody and would rather die than to runaway. Eva is the only thing that made her feel like a somebody and would rather die than to abandon the only purpose in life she knows of. She insults others in order to make her existence feel more significant but she only does so in order to lessen the great amount of pain inside her. She has a antagonistic type of personality on the outside but I always felt that she was a very sweet person in the inside and it showed in some scenes. She has the type of drive to never give up until the end. Something I longed for and finally achieved! I will talk about Rei and Kaworu later.

[]:             Quote Post by Darkwing » Sat Dec 22, 2012 8:33 am I gotta disagree with you about Asuka. She does give up at one point. She's the one who ended up in the bathtub, and catatonic. She breaks, and she breaks hard. When she loses everything, she just sorta stops. Until she gets put back in the Eva, realizes she doesn't want to die, and finds her mother in there, and that she loves her. So, no Asuka isn't always able to persevere. However she does get better. That's the thing. Asuka falls the farthest, but really she was always worse than she seemed. At the end she climbs back up, and finds a renewed sense of self. Then the fucking harpies eat her. I hate those things.

[]:             Quote Post by CaasiBoy » Sat Dec 22, 2012 9:27 am I just came to say I love this thread. Sorry. Not much to add, at least that hasn't already been said.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva-UnitK » Sat Dec 22, 2012 11:14 am You are correct! I can't believe that I've forgotten that segment. But I mean come on if she was forced to relive a horrible experience from her childhood then her only known purpose in life was taken from her! There was no one that really understood her during that time no one to consult with and worst of all she was saved by Rei! Rei is the exact opposite of everything that Asuka was trying to be. She viewed her as a doll and hated her even more in the fact of her saying she would kill herself if ordered to. Now she was saved by someone who doesn't even care about her own life! This greatly hurts Asuka's pride and also another fact I believe that causes her breakdown was how she wanted Shinji to be the one to save her or at least help her but the poor guy couldn't do anything. Asuka's defenses are basically gone and doesn't care about anything anymore since she views herself as just a nuisance to the others now. So she just gave up because, everything that she worked for was ruined. Thus leading to the bath scene. Also I agree on you about those MP Evas. Shaming her without even fighting back and wait for her most vulnerable moment and devour her?! Fucking Cowards!

[]:             Quote Post by Na7e » Sat Dec 22, 2012 12:10 pm Darkwing wrote: At the end she climbs back up, and finds a renewed sense of self. Then the fucking harpies eat her. I hate those things.

[]:             Quote Post by Darkwing » Sat Dec 22, 2012 8:51 pm Eva-UnitK wrote: You are correct! I can't believe that I've forgotten that segment. But I mean come on if she was forced to relive a horrible experience from her childhood then her only known purpose in life was taken from her! There was no one that really understood her during that time no one to consult with and worst of all she was saved by Rei! Rei is the exact opposite of everything that Asuka was trying to be. This greatly hurts Asuka's pride and also another fact I believe that causes her breakdown was how she wanted Shinji to be the one to save her or at least help her but the poor guy couldn't do anything. Asuka's defenses are basically gone and doesn't care about anything anymore since she views herself as just a nuisance to the others now. So she just gave up because, everything that she worked for was ruined. Thus leading to the bath scene. Also I agree on you about those MP Evas. Shaming her without even fighting back and wait for her most vulnerable moment and devour her?! Fucking Cowards!
summary: []: What do you find most appealing about the Eva-pilots?             Quote Post by Write » Fri Dec 21, 2012 5:31 am I'm writing a story (I have aspirations for the story but they're unimportant right now) and I'd like to create the most appealing characters possible, even if only by design. I hope I get a lot of replies. Evangelion's fan base is particularly important to me as my story has it's share of [indirec

chunks.content:
Article: What do you find most appealing about the Eva pilots
[]: What do you find most appealing about the Eva-pilots?             Quote Post by Write » Fri Dec 21, 2012 5:31 am I'm writing a story (I have aspirations for the story but they're unimportant right now) and I'd like to create the most appealing characters possible, even if only by design. I hope I get a lot of replies. Evangelion's fan base is particularly important to me as my story has it's share of [indirect] influences. It's not mecha or anything like that, but it's worth noting that I was inspired by Hideaki Anno and the love people have for his characters when they're not inherently appealing (or maybe they are, that's why I'm asking).

Article: What do you find most appealing about the Eva pilots
 like Shinji, but can't admit it. You actually do want to see them grow and gain happiness. Now don't assume that it's a case of "oh look at the poor woobie, I wish they were happy". []:             Quote Post by Nyanlathotep » Fri Dec 21, 2012 12:30 pm A good phrase from Anno describes what I like about them: They have blood. Like, even in most other stories where the characters are emotionally damaged, there are still bits and pieces of their personalities that are subdued so as to make the characters inoffensive and easy to watch. The characters, in their emotional pains, can get really hard to watch, just like watching a good friend have an emotional breakdown. Anno, more than any other director I've seen, nails the pathos of very disturbed characters. This is because the characters are portrayed extremely honestly, and while there is depth to them, a lot of that depth ultimately comes to the surface in a powerful way as the characters reach their boiling points. Also, Anno has a knack for punishing character judgment and hatred. Think Asuka's a bitch? Here's why she's the way she is; I hope you have your Zoloft ready. Think Gendou is cold and manipulative because he's a psychopath? Psyche! His mind has been ruined by an obsession with his dead* wife and a crippling fear of connecting with anyone else-- he simply tries to cope with his duress by treating the people around him as objects.

[]:             Quote Post by Rei IV » Fri Dec 21, 2012 12:54 pm They all suffer from some form of self-hatred and have identity issues to boot. Some of the character's deficiencies are also very similar which brings back to their self hatred.

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Fri Dec 21, 2012 1:10 pm They're all flawed. That's something too many authors forget to do when writing characters. They get so wrapped up in trying to make them likable that they forget to add flaws, they become too two dimensional, and the result is they're less lik

Article: What do you find most appealing about the Eva pilots
're all flawed. That's something too many authors forget to do when writing characters. They get so wrapped up in trying to make them likable that they forget to add flaws, they become too two dimensional, and the result is they're less likable because of it. Just don't feel obligated to fuck your characters up as much as Anno did.

[]:             Quote Post by Tribblepoo » Fri Dec 21, 2012 3:22 pm CJD wrote: They're all flawed. That's something too many authors forget to do when writing characters.

[]:             Quote Post by Darkwing » Fri Dec 21, 2012 5:41 pm CJD wrote: They're all flawed. That's something too many authors forget to do when writing characters. They get so wrapped up in trying to make them likable that they forget to add flaws, they become too two dimensional, and the result is they're less likable because of it. Just don't feel obligated to fuck your characters up as much as Anno did.

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Fri Dec 21, 2012 6:25 pm I figured "including virtues too" went without saying guys. How often do you encounter a character who has no virtues? Compare that to how often you encounter a Mary Sue? Including virtues is easy, most people do it by default, the hard part is including believable flaws. Going off "believable," I'd like to reiterate something I've said before (most recently the other day): The difference between Shinji and the angst ridden protagonists in a lot of shows lately is that Shinji has a reason to act the way he does. Shinji's issues are believable because of his past. Don't just throw in flaws for the sake of flaws, make them believable character traits.

[]:             Quote Post by Nyanlathotep » Fri Dec 21, 2012 6:26 pm Not only do they have flaws, they have real flaws-- flaws that can't just be brushed aside or conveniently reconciled. That's another thing writers and narrative artists tend to forget. EDIT: Also, going from above-- the characters also have realistic and flaws. A big goal of art in general is that you want everything to come across as justified within the story.

 also have realistic and flaws. A big goal of art in general is that you want everything to come across as justified within the story.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Fri Dec 21, 2012 6:30 pm I guess first of all, it's the sheer level of detail and artistic beauty Anno put into them. Down to philosophic opinions and hobbies and everything, and it all fits together/tells you something about them/ flows from the same basic traits. There are just many aspets/layers of meanings to them, just like a real person is not "just one thing", you can really get a feel for their inner universes... A problem with creating characters is the balance between making them represent things, abstract concepts for your message and still feel like real people - Obviously, Anno aced that department, as much as the mystery/relatability axis or the question of what and how much to reveal. Realism has been mentioned before - Eva's writing is just very lifelike, I often heard the dialogue and thought "Heck, I had that same conversation with my mother/classmates a while ago!". They're modelled after basic types of people that our modern sucess-oriented society produces, and obviously, the audience may recognize these types, leading to strong reactions of "That poor girl/boy is just like me! I feel your pain, [insert character here]! " or "Yes, I know this kind of asshole from my own life and I can't stand them!" Sometimes, they might do things you wouldn't expect of them, and that challenges you to remedy your model/theory of their inner mechanics (For me, it was mostly Shinji I had that experience with, but many mention having it with Asuka) One trick is probably that he gave most of the characters, not just the pilots, a mixture of very positive and very negative traits, or traits that can both positive and negative depending on the situation they come out, not the usual 2-3 good and one bad trait model used most frequently. In general, an apparent contradiction can be a good thing to base an interesting, memorable character about. (For example, Sadamoto (or was it Anno? Not sure, i think it was the former) once said that his first idea of Ritsuko was "a miniskirt half-concealed under a lab-coat". Or take Gendo. A hard, extremely pragmatic, un-sentimental leader ...

Article: What do you find most appealing about the Eva pilots
 think it was the former) once said that his first idea of Ritsuko was "a miniskirt half-concealed under a lab-coat". Or take Gendo. A hard, extremely pragmatic, un-sentimental leader ... who regurlarly visits an empty grave. Misato, the cool, professional, hypercompetent pretty babe... who's a drunkyard living in a filthy appartment. Shinji. Recklessly charges the monster against direct orders, puts a knife in it the most primal way possible, and just keeps going no matter what that thing does to him, screaming like a mad ape, taking it down at the last second, also saving two involving civilians and the rest of the planet ...and then breaks down crying, reduced to a complete wreck. ) As far as the pilots in particular are concerned, well, why'd Anno use 14-year-olds. Simple: Not too old to angst over parents, old enough to angst about misadventures with the opposite sex. And because they're still naturally in a process of develeloping, that makes them ideal for a story about a "journey" In addition to that, they all sorta have their own appeal, after all, not everyone likes them all and some like some of them while strongly disliking certain others. The people who like both Shinji and Asuka probably make for one small list XD Shinji: a) Realism. As mentioned before, that's a factor with most of EVAs cast, but Shinji is the ordinary PoV character who gets dragged into that weird world of mecha battles. He's so very, very human, for better or for worse. And his struggles/flaws are very accurate depictions of what some actual unhappy teenagers go through. People are gonna recognize that, and react. b) Fragility appeal. Poor thing, don't you feel like hugging him just from listening to his voice for too long? He's such a nice, guy, too, and even if he wavers, even if he considers quitting, in the end, he keeps continueing his struggles despite the pain. That makes it way more satisfying to see him succeed at something than your average hero, because it's a lot harder to him and will mean a lot more to/for him. c) Conversely, he still manages to keep some mysterious blanks in his backstory and has a very unpredictable quality to him, a hidden core of anger, something powerful and abyssal, terrifying

Article: What do you find most appealing about the Eva pilots
 to him and will mean a lot more to/for him. c) Conversely, he still manages to keep some mysterious blanks in his backstory and has a very unpredictable quality to him, a hidden core of anger, something powerful and abyssal, terrifying that's fascinating and awesome to behold, but may just be something you can't coexist with. So he has the appeal of a fairly repressed, clean and nice-seeming guy with a hidden black soul lurking in his depths. d) Most non-stereotypical bisexual character ever. Rei (mostly second version) a) Fragility appeal, once again. The basic feeling of maybe being needed, maybe being able to win the loyalty of a even a distant-seeming person if you only try and show them kindness. The instinct to protect. She evokes that like no other, and it helps that we see the story through the eyes of someone who feels the same way about her. Her VA always makes her sound like she's about to faint, we see her sad, sad living conditions and the terrible knowledge she's forced to live with. She care's far too little about herself and her own wishes, her very life, her modesty, and so, you want to do it for her. b) She's a challenge for your brainz. Piecing together what the deal is, all the philosophic mojo involved, what we see her ask herself, and what she makes us ponder. The meaning of identity, communication and hope, basically. c) Were you ever a real life socially clueless young girl? Rei's life is gonna seem like a big metaphor for yours. Several scenes there felt ripped right out of my life.... "I know that pain"-sort of appeal, once again. d) There's also the expressionist concept of your life an an individual human feeling insignificant in the modern world, the coldness and sterility of science/civilization/ big cities, the anonimity and the feeling that it wouldn't really matter if one person more or less were there. e) But conversely, seeing her story may convince you that your life can be improved if it sucks like hers. She has worse reasons for her life sucking and her feeling the way she does than you probably have, but even she found a friend. And she manages to be a kind, determined, honest and loyal person despite it all. That makes her someone to admire, I guess, that silent streght she has. She worries about her teammates and holds no grudges

Article: What do you find most appealing about the Eva pilots
 a friend. And she manages to be a kind, determined, honest and loyal person despite it all. That makes her someone to admire, I guess, that silent streght she has. She worries about her teammates and holds no grudges, but will not suffer her loved ones being insulted. She's honest, to the point of being brutal with it, she doesn't hide herself and that's beautiful. Her personality also makes her a cute and likeable character that you want to see happy, I guess. The appeal of the third version is mostly of the SciFi/ philosophic sort, but her character arc also has aspects comparable to the human wish/struggle for freedom from opression and going one's own way/ expression of free will, or a daghter's wish to be independent from her father and his plans for her, and it's strangely powerful to see such a very human, relatable sentiment in a mostly in-human character who sees her existence as a wrong, undesirable state in a way we can't understand. An interesting exploration how she's mentally/metaphisically a half-human-hybrid, not just in body. The whole theme with the clones and how they differ despite having the same bodies/ same memories is also a nice exploration of what makes a human identity and what happens when you twist the various ingedients. It's beautiful, it's just beautiful, how both mayor clones were ultimately just themselves, not Lillith, not Yui, not just their upbringing in a lab, not just the purpose intended for them by Gendo. ReiIIs connection with Shinji was ultimately hers and hers alone, a teenaged girl becoming friends with or developing a crush on another teenager, drawn together because they knew the same loneliness. Yui or Lillith would see him as their son, not as someone equal or a source of comfort, and certainly not a potential love interest. And ReiIIIs final choice to defy Gendo was also hers, not her predecessors, hers alone. Even if her choice entailed giving up her indentity, it was hers... Fuck, I'm getting teary-eyed just writing about it. You see? Beautiful. Kaworu: a) Intelligent, kind, devoted, can read people like books and seems to understand everything. He's hot, and he likes music and philosophy. What's not to like? Don't we all wish to meet such a person? b) His musings about human nature, it's limitations, but also the

Article: What do you find most appealing about the Eva pilots
 and seems to understand everything. He's hot, and he likes music and philosophy. What's not to like? Don't we all wish to meet such a person? b) His musings about human nature, it's limitations, but also the beauty in it. I was never the same after listening to them. He just looks like such a shining light when he appears in the dark, empty world of evangelion's fourth act, so positive, so enlightened, and he, a stranger from the outside who's not part of humanity, makes you appreciate it through new eyes. Episode 24 makes my soul happy. It makes me wish that maybe I could be like him, it makes me want to try. Because he was used and betrayed by SEELE, he's an artificial being just like Rei, he's agonizing about big choices just like Shinji, he knows his days in the world around him are numbered, but instead of agonizing over it like the rest of the cast, he choses to enjoy what he has, he goes forward and connects to people. I find Kaworu Nagisa inspiring beyond words and i tell you, episode 24 changed my life. c) I guess also SciFi/cerebral reasons, as with Rei. Mari: a) Somewhat like Kaworu, there are implications that her past sucked, but she has a positive attitude. She knows that she's weird, but she has no complex about it, and got herself a situation where she can be the way she is and make use of it, too. b) Bad. Ass. I usually don't care for fight scenes, but... it's not even that she always wins or is super skilled, it's the way she's ready tp utilize everything in her grasp. c) Subversive Element in a way perfect for the setting. In a world full of lies and secrets, she is the one who knows, and escapes the tangles webs of fate, unlike the people who keep walking right into them... d) That "dissonant serenity" of hers. Asuka: ...Hm, I guess someone who actually likes her in an emotional way can explain that better than me. Here's what I see about her: a) Escapism. She tells people around her they suck without a moments hesitantion and is very complainy. Half of us wish we could, but we're afraid of the repercussions. So watching her can provise catharsis, especially if the plot untill episode 8

Article: What do you find most appealing about the Eva pilots
 people around her they suck without a moments hesitantion and is very complainy. Half of us wish we could, but we're afraid of the repercussions. So watching her can provise catharsis, especially if the plot untill episode 8 annoyed you. ...I guess. I can't like her, the way she treats Rei reminds me so much of when I used to be bullied when I was younger, but I can see her more cerebral attempts, the point of this character, and the message. IT's comparable to Marina's "Electra Heart" album, I guess. This character adresses issues present in our society, she represents a type or types of people that our society produces, with the drive to be perfect, beautiful and popular. Surrounded by pictures of "perfect" bodies, ware that there's always someone better for work and school. b) She has to deal with the common situation of her parent's divorce, a mother who's said to have put her work before her and ultimstely dies for it, leaving Asuka behind, not recognizing her. It's probably an exxageration for, well, workaholic mothers than don't die, but still don't pay much attention to their children. And the whole "Daddy doesn't love mommy anymore. Does that mean he regrets having me"-complex and the stepmother situation ("Want to have a mother, but feel like I have to reject that person because she represents Daddy's betrayal") I guess that if your parents divorced and you didn't agree with it (I'd be happy if my mother finally got rid of her asshole husband, more like Misato) , too, episodes 22 and 25 are gonna make you bawl, dunno. In any case, it's adressing a problem people may know. c) Her whole perfectionism complex, and breakdown it leads to. Asuka is essentially someone who gets themselves a burnout syndrome.It's a problem people have. She makes rivals of perople who were never too different from her and could've been allies. She's a lone fighter, she feels like it's her against the world and of course it hurts/is too much for her to take on the whole word. She needs to be perfect, and so, she breaks what could have been not perfect, but good. she could do it all better if she just relaxed, but something happened to her to make her need the recognition of being elite, to crave the stagelight and attention like a drug,

Article: What do you find most appealing about the Eva pilots
, she breaks what could have been not perfect, but good. she could do it all better if she just relaxed, but something happened to her to make her need the recognition of being elite, to crave the stagelight and attention like a drug, and it becomes a vicious circle. d) related to the drivbe to be perfect in a professional/academic way, but still very different, the drive to be "socially" perfect, to have a perfect body. She seems very petty and superficial at first, concerded with silly things like "winning" or "appearances" and making fun of the "losers" around her, and sometimes she gets very cruel there, but it's only to mask her insecurities, as it it often in real life. She's projecting a fake image that prevents her connections from being meaningful, because no one knows her real self, so it'd doesn't really help her loneliness. Asuka is shown to be vain, ("Look at my curves!""No way I'm wearing that silly suit..." ) and she stands out as being very thin in an anime where the drawing styles makes everyone noodles to begin with, and she ends up gaunt and emaciated, in that filthy bathtub. Compare/contrast classic story of you average anorexia victim. She's that "alpha bitch" girl who goes and viciously bullies outsiders like Rei, and pushes around less confident individuals (like Shinji) to do her bidding, but as in real life bullying, there's a lot of projecting involved. Episode 25: "You are trying to find yourself in others..." She sees weakness in herself, but she can't accept it, so she hates others who remind her of it, hates it in others. She looks for people who seem to be more miserable than her to make herself feel better. Of course, this makes her have very simplistic, carricaturized perceptions of others. She assigns them roles in "her" story, the tragicomic play that her life is and where even she is an actress playing the role of a simplistic carricature, and so, she doesn't get to know their real selves, doesn't get to see their real feelings that she is hurting. She calls Misato a slut, Shinji a weakling and Rei an obedient mechanical puppet, but in truth, it's Asuka herself who has self-doubts and is afraid of being a slut, a weakling or an obedient mechanical puppet. She wants to reject these things

Article: What do you find most appealing about the Eva pilots
 Shinji a weakling and Rei an obedient mechanical puppet, but in truth, it's Asuka herself who has self-doubts and is afraid of being a slut, a weakling or an obedient mechanical puppet. She wants to reject these things, separate them from herself. e) But it's also the story of a naive young girl who thought she was prepared shattering upon the hard rocks of reality and to come to term with being a woman of all sudden. She acts as if she were confident with her role and her appeal,, giving her firsts away like they're something shameful to have, not something precious to keep, but in fact, she's insecure about her ability to win the hearts of men and fails to connect with people who are already women and could guide her. She's ve ry childish and selfish in some ways, but she doesn't really realize. She was a child-woman complex, throwing herself on an older man, but in the end, she's a lonely little girl who failed to become a woman. She's uncomfortable with her own ability to give life. Or partially suceeds in 2.0, it's a matter of debate, and don't get me started on Q, but yeah. That part of the character gets adressed in a different way there. You could say that EoE has her come to full circle, or that it doesn't, but that's a theme there. So in many ways, she's very relevant, and I guess that makes her appealing... again, I tried my best but you should better ask an Asuka fan thereXD I absolutely love the other four, though. I love them to bits. But I guess part of the point is, a character that adresses relevant issues is more likely to be good and make the fans talk about them.

[]:             Quote Post by Darkwing » Fri Dec 21, 2012 9:36 pm Actually I think the pilots being 14 years old was because most Mecha protagonists are about that age. Well that's what it says on the bio. They don't often act that way. And that was one of the major deconstructions of Eva. You actually put 14 year old kids in these things, what's going to happen, and whats it going to do to them. How it'll put pressure on them. And where they'll break. Now for me the episodes between Asuka's arrival and Leliel are actually important. There were we get

Article: What do you find most appealing about the Eva pilots
's going to happen, and whats it going to do to them. How it'll put pressure on them. And where they'll break. Now for me the episodes between Asuka's arrival and Leliel are actually important. There were we get to just see everyone being themselves. There's some little charecter moments (though I think Rei could have used a few more), and we do learn a little bit. It's the calm before the storm. It gives us a chance to get comfortable with the charecters before it all goes to hell. If you don't care about the charecters you won't feel for them when they get put through hell. And lets face it if the creators did a good job then you will care about the charecters, for good or ill. -Shinji. At times I want to hug the kid, tell him he did good and that he's a good person, and he just needs to keep working on things, he'll get it eventually. Other times I want to scream at him in frustration because he's such a screw up, and yell at him that his father is not worth getting emotionally eaten alive over. -Rei. She's the one I want to see bloom. Come out into the world and find out what it means to be happy. To see not just smile, but laugh. And in some ways she's the saddest charecter to me because it never has a chance to happen. It gets nipped in the bud right as it was begining. As for Rei 3, there is always a sense of unease. But at the same time I wish she'd understand that death is not something to long for or to fear. -Asuka. Now here's someone at times I want to choke. But I really do like to see it in those rare times when she allows herself to be something other than the alpha bitch who is invincible. Not just her vulnerable moments, but her very rare kind moments. Like deciding they were going for Ramen instead of steak so Rei could join them. I want to scream at her "Of course no one likes you! You don't like yourself! Stop trying to force everything so you can pretend it doesn't hurt!" And of course at the same time I feel for her, that poor little girl who didn't just lose her mother, but saw her descend into insanity. And who was smart enough to know what the doll means... -Toji. The dumb j

Article: What do you find most appealing about the Eva pilots
 And of course at the same time I feel for her, that poor little girl who didn't just lose her mother, but saw her descend into insanity. And who was smart enough to know what the doll means... -Toji. The dumb jock. Only he's not. On the surface I probably would never have hung out with a jock like him in highschool. or maybe I would have, since Kensuke does. He comes across like a dumb lunk at first. But really he's got so many good traits. He loves his sister very much. He has a sense of honor. He hit Shinji when Shinji didn't deserve it, so he needs Shinji to hit him back. He has that kindness Hikari sees, and that attracts her. And then there is a great scene that sadly is only in the manga. He tells Shinji that he's the 4th child and breaks down in tears because he's so scared. And really any kid his age suddenly going into that should. Espescially since he's seen what it's like for Shinji. -Misato. A damn capable operations officier, and as we see in EOE, a dangerous woman. But at the same time she's as bad in her personal life as the children. She doesn't know if she loves or hates her father. She managed to screw up her relationship with the man she loves because of that. Her best freind? At times the two can't stand each other, and really she doesn't know her that well. The scar on her chest isn't the only one she's got. -Kawrou. If I had to liken him to any other fictional character I'd say The Doctor. For whatever reason he loves humanity. And he loves Shinji who really embodies the human condition. But at the same time he's always sort of distant and different. Of all the angels, only he really resembles what most people would call an angel, and I don't just mean looks. -Gendo. Oh don't get me started. I'll be here all night. Suffice it to say I find him both pityable and pitifule. Sympathetic, and contemptable. But it says tons that I can find so very much to say about the characters.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva-UnitK » Sat Dec 22, 2012 3:48 am Well what I find most appealing is how realistic the characters feel and how I can relate to them. Shinji: Shinji is basically a perfect representation of how I was in my early days of adolescence. Like him I felt like I had no freedom on anything and pretty much didn't care about my own existence. I was angry the whole time during those years and just kept my feelings bottled up and never consulted with anyone for help. So I completely understands his reasons for his actions and I also understand his own hatred of himself. Asuka: The reason why I like Asuka so much is because of her ability to keep moving forward and still have the will to live even after witnessing what she's been through. She never backs away and always tries to be in the spotlight all the time in order to show the world that she exists. Heck she even went as far as finishing school in Germany all the way to her college graduation! She desperately wanted to be known and she also felt that she needs to do these things to praise herself and erase the sadness in her heart. Though probably more mentally unstable than the other pilots are she still tried to be the most normal of the trio to be around. Something that I couldn't do even if I tried. She doesn't want to be a insignificant nobody and would rather die than to runaway. Eva is the only thing that made her feel like a somebody and would rather die than to abandon the only purpose in life she knows of. She insults others in order to make her existence feel more significant but she only does so in order to lessen the great amount of pain inside her. She has a antagonistic type of personality on the outside but I always felt that she was a very sweet person in the inside and it showed in some scenes. She has the type of drive to never give up until the end. Something I longed for and finally achieved! I will talk about Rei and Kaworu later.

She viewed her as a doll and hated her even more in the fact of her saying she would kill herself if ordered to. Now she was saved by someone who doesn't even care about her own life! This greatly hurts Asuka's pride and also another fact I believe that causes her breakdown was how she wanted Shinji to be the one to save her or at least help her but the poor guy couldn't do anything. Asuka's defenses are basically gone and doesn't care about anything anymore since she views herself as just a nuisance to the others now. So she just gave up because, everything that she worked for was ruined. Thus leading to the bath scene. Also I agree on you about those MP E
